result.markdown:
--- title: "From Linear Regression to Deep Learning" subtitle: "A step-by-step journey through the building blocks of neural networks" author: "Andreas Bogossian" --- # From Linear Regression to Deep Learning > **The big idea:** A deep neural network is not magic — it is linear regression, repeated, stacked, and bent into non-linearity. Every concept builds naturally on the last. In this post we take a single idea — predicting an output from inputs — and gradually extend it until we arrive at a feedforward neural network. At each step we pause to visualise the current state of the story. --- ## 1. Simple Linear Regression The simplest model imaginable: one input $x$, one output $\hat{y}$, connected by a line. $$\hat{y} = w \cdot x + b$$ - $w$ — the **weight** (slope of the line) - $b$ — the **bias** (intercept) We fit the model by minimising the **Mean Squared Error** (MSE): $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$ ::: {#022940e4 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt rng = np.random.default_rng(42) x = rng.uniform(0, 10, 80) y = 2.5 * x + 1.2 + rng.normal(0, 2, 80) w, b = np.polyfit(x, y, 1) x_line = np.linspace(0, 10, 200) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4)) ax.scatter(x, y, alpha=0.5, color="#4C72B0", label="Data") ax.plot(x_line, w * x_line + b, color="#C44E52", lw=2, label=f"$\\hat{{y}} = {w:.2f}x + {b:.2f}$") ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("ŷ") ax.set_title("Simple Linear Regression") ax.legend(); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show() print(f"Learned: w = {w:.3f}, b = {b:.3f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-2-output-1.png){width=663 height=374} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Learned: w = 2.593, b = 0.482 ``` ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Architecture so far ```{=html}
``` A single input, multiplied by a weight, shifted by a bias, producing one output. That is our foundation. --- ## 2. Multiple Linear Regression Now let's allow **multiple inputs** $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_p$. Each gets its own weight: $$\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_p x_p + b = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$ This is still a linear function — we've only added more inputs. In matrix form for a dataset of $n$ rows: $$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} + b$$ ::: {#bfd42c7f .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler rng = np.random.default_rng(0) X = rng.uniform(0, 10, (200, 3)) y_multi = 1.5*X[:,0] - 0.8*X[:,1] + 2.1*X[:,2] + 0.5 + rng.normal(0, 1.5, 200) scaler = StandardScaler() X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X) lr = LinearRegression().fit(X_scaled, y_multi) print("Weights:", np.round(lr.coef_, 3)) print("Bias: ", round(lr.intercept_, 3)) print(f"R²: {lr.score(X_scaled, y_multi):.3f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Weights: [ 4.406 -2.235 6.334] Bias: 14.642 R²: 0.966 ``` ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Architecture so far ```{=html}
``` Multiple inputs, still one output, still a linear model. Every arrow carries its own weight. --- ## 3. Logistic Regression — Adding a Non-Linearity What if $y$ is a **class label** (0 or 1)? A raw linear output can be any real number — not useful as a probability. We wrap it in the **sigmoid function**: $$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$ $$\hat{p} = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$$ The sigmoid squashes any value into $(0, 1)$. This is our **first activation function** — a taste of what comes next. ::: {#e31a4c43 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} from sklearn.datasets import make_classification from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score X_cls, y_cls = make_classification(n_samples=300, n_features=2, n_redundant=0, n_informative=2, random_state=1) clf = LogisticRegression().fit(X_cls, y_cls) # Decision boundary xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-3, 3, 300), np.linspace(-3, 3, 300)) Z = clf.predict_proba(np.c_[xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()])[:,1].reshape(xx.shape) fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4)) axes[0].contourf(xx, yy, Z, levels=20, cmap="RdBu_r", alpha=0.7) axes[0].scatter(X_cls[:,0], X_cls[:,1], c=y_cls, cmap="RdBu_r", edgecolors="k", s=25) axes[0].set_title("Logistic Regression — decision boundary") axes[0].set_xlabel("x₁"); axes[0].set_ylabel("x₂") z_vals = np.linspace(-6, 6, 200) axes[1].plot(z_vals, 1/(1+np.exp(-z_vals)), color="#4C72B0", lw=2) axes[1].axhline(0.5, color="#C44E52", lw=1, linestyle="--", label="threshold 0.5") axes[1].set_title("Sigmoid activation σ(z)") axes[1].set_xlabel("z (linear combination)"); axes[1].set_ylabel("σ(z)") axes[1].legend(); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show() print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_cls, clf.predict(X_cls)):.3f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-4-output-1.png){width=1046 height=374} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Accuracy: 0.897 ``` ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Architecture so far ```{=html}
``` We now have the full anatomy of a **single artificial neuron**: a linear combination followed by a non-linear activation function. --- ## 4. A Single Neuron (Perceptron) Let's generalise what we have. A single **neuron** with $p$ inputs: $$a = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j x_j + b\right)$$ This is the perceptron. Every neuron in every neural network is exactly this — a weighted sum of its inputs, passed through an activation function. Common activation functions: | Name | Formula | Range | |------|---------|-------| | Sigmoid | $\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | | Tanh | $\tanh(z)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | | ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | | Leaky ReLU | $\max(\alpha z, z)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | ::: {#1431753c .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} z = np.linspace(-4, 4, 300) activations = { "Sigmoid": 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z)), "Tanh": np.tanh(z), "ReLU": np.maximum(0, z), "Leaky ReLU": np.where(z >= 0, z, 0.1 * z), } colors = ["#4C72B0", "#DD8452", "#55A868", "#C44E52"] fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4)) for (name, vals), color in zip(activations.items(), colors): ax.plot(z, vals, label=name, color=color, lw=2) ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.5) ax.set_title("Common Activation Functions") ax.set_xlabel("z"); ax.set_ylabel("activation") ax.legend(); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-5-output-1.png){width=758 height=374} ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Architecture so far ```{=html}
``` One neuron is already a complete model. Now we scale up. --- ## 5. A Layer of Neurons What if we place **multiple neurons side by side**, each taking all inputs? We get a **dense layer** (also called a fully-connected layer). For a layer with $h$ neurons and $p$ inputs: $$\mathbf{a} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$$ - $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times p}$ — weight matrix - $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — bias vector - $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — activation vector (the layer's output) ::: {#25fdb42e .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} import torch import torch.nn as nn # A single dense layer: 3 inputs → 4 hidden neurons layer = nn.Linear(3, 4) x_demo = torch.tensor([[1.2, -0.5, 0.9]]) output = torch.relu(layer(x_demo)) print("Weight matrix W (4×3):\n", layer.weight.data.numpy().round(3)) print("\nBias vector b (4,):\n", layer.bias.data.numpy().round(3)) print("\nActivation output (4,):\n", output.data.numpy().round(3)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Weight matrix W (4×3): [[-0.149 0.066 -0.194] [ 0.55 0.528 -0.449] [-0.279 -0.229 0.006] [-0.054 -0.448 -0.5 ]] Bias vector b (4,): [-0.06 -0.374 -0.568 -0.39 ] Activation output (4,): [[0. 0. 0. 0.]] ``` ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Architecture so far ```{=html}
``` Every input connects to every neuron in the layer — hence *fully connected*. The weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ holds all the connection strengths. --- ## 6. Stacking Layers — The Deep Part One hidden layer is fine. But **stacking multiple layers** lets the network learn hierarchical features. This is literally what "deep" learning means. $$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}\right)$$ $$\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(2)}\mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}\right)$$ $$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = f_{\text{out}}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(3)}\mathbf{a}^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}^{(3)}\right)$$ The key insight: **each layer transforms the representation** produced by the previous one. The network learns to build up abstract features automatically. ::: {#f070fd1e .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} net = nn.Sequential( nn.Linear(3, 8), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(8, 4), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(4, 1), ) print(net) total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in net.parameters()) print(f"\nTotal trainable parameters: {total_params}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Sequential( (0): Linear(in_features=3, out_features=8, bias=True) (1): ReLU() (2): Linear(in_features=8, out_features=4, bias=True) (3): ReLU() (4): Linear(in_features=4, out_features=1, bias=True) ) Total trainable parameters: 73 ``` ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Architecture so far ```{=html}
``` We now have a multi-layer network. This is already the full **deep feedforward architecture**. --- ## 7. Training — Backpropagation and Gradient Descent Having a deep network is useless without a way to **learn** the weights. Training requires two things: ### 7.1 A Loss Function For regression: MSE. For classification: **Cross-entropy loss**: $$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)$$ ### 7.2 Gradient Descent We want to minimise the loss w.r.t. all weights. Gradient descent updates weights iteratively: $$\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}$$ where $\eta$ is the **learning rate**. ### 7.3 Backpropagation The key algorithm: efficiently compute $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}$ using the **chain rule**, propagating error gradients from output layer back to input layer. $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(k)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(k)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(k)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(k)}}$$ ::: {#19582551 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Training a small network on XOR — the classic problem linear models can't solve import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.optim as optim X_xor = torch.tensor([[0.,0.],[0.,1.],[1.,0.],[1.,1.]]) y_xor = torch.tensor([[0.],[1.],[1.],[0.]]) model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(2,4), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(4,1), nn.Sigmoid()) optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.05) criterion = nn.BCELoss() losses = [] for epoch in range(2000): pred = model(X_xor) loss = criterion(pred, y_xor) optimizer.zero_grad() loss.backward() # backpropagation! optimizer.step() if epoch % 100 == 0: losses.append(loss.item()) fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4)) axes[0].plot(range(0, 2000, 100), losses, color="#4C72B0", lw=2) axes[0].set_title("Training Loss (XOR)"); axes[0].set_xlabel("Epoch"); axes[0].set_ylabel("BCE Loss") xx2, yy2 = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-0.5,1.5,200), np.linspace(-0.5,1.5,200)) grid = torch.tensor(np.c_[xx2.ravel(), yy2.ravel()], dtype=torch.float32) with torch.no_grad(): Z2 = model(grid).numpy().reshape(xx2.shape) axes[1].contourf(xx2, yy2, Z2, levels=20, cmap="RdBu_r", alpha=0.8) axes[1].scatter(X_xor[:,0], X_xor[:,1], c=y_xor[:,0], cmap="RdBu_r", s=120, edgecolors="k", zorder=5) axes[1].set_title("Learned XOR Decision Boundary") axes[1].set_xlabel("x₁"); axes[1].set_ylabel("x₂") plt.tight_layout(); plt.show() with torch.no_grad(): preds = (model(X_xor) > 0.5).float() print("Predictions:", preds.T.numpy()) print("Expected: ", y_xor.T.numpy()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-8-output-1.png){width=950 height=374} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Predictions: [[0. 1. 1. 0.]] Expected: [[0. 1. 1. 0.]] ``` ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Architecture so far — with gradient flow ```{=html}
``` The green arrows carry data **forward** through the network. The red dashed arrows carry gradients **backward** — this is backpropagation. Every weight is nudged in the direction that reduces the loss. --- ## 8. The Complete Feedforward Neural Network We now have everything. Let's put it together properly and train it on a real classification task. $$ \boxed{ \text{Feedforward Neural Network} = \text{stacked layers of} \; \mathbf{a}^{(k)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(k)}\mathbf{a}^{(k-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(k)}\right) } $$ ::: {#fc74d213 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} from sklearn.datasets import make_moons from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler # Dataset X_m, y_m = make_moons(n_samples=600, noise=0.25, random_state=42) X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_m, y_m, test_size=0.2, random_state=0) scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X_train) X_train_s = scaler.transform(X_train) X_test_s = scaler.transform(X_test) X_tr = torch.tensor(X_train_s, dtype=torch.float32) y_tr = torch.tensor(y_train, dtype=torch.float32).unsqueeze(1) X_te = torch.tensor(X_test_s, dtype=torch.float32) y_te = torch.tensor(y_test, dtype=torch.float32).unsqueeze(1) # Full feedforward network ffn = nn.Sequential( nn.Linear(2, 16), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(16, 8), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(8, 1), nn.Sigmoid() ) optimizer = optim.Adam(ffn.parameters(), lr=0.01) criterion = nn.BCELoss() history = [] for epoch in range(1500): ffn.train() pred = ffn(X_tr) loss = criterion(pred, y_tr) optimizer.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optimizer.step() if epoch % 50 == 0: ffn.eval() with torch.no_grad(): val_loss = criterion(ffn(X_te), y_te).item() history.append((epoch, loss.item(), val_loss)) # Visualise results history = np.array(history) fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5)) axes[0].plot(history[:,0], history[:,1], label="Train loss", color="#4C72B0", lw=2) axes[0].plot(history[:,0], history[:,2], label="Val loss", color="#DD8452", lw=2, linestyle="--") axes[0].set_title("Training Curve — Feedforward Network") axes[0].set_xlabel("Epoch"); axes[0].set_ylabel("BCE Loss") axes[0].legend() xx3, yy3 = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-3,3,300), np.linspace(-3,3,300)) grid3 = torch.tensor(np.c_[xx3.ravel(), yy3.ravel()], dtype=torch.float32) ffn.eval() with torch.no_grad(): Z3 = ffn(grid3).numpy().reshape(xx3.shape) axes[1].contourf(xx3, yy3, Z3, levels=20, cmap="RdBu_r", alpha=0.75) axes[1].scatter(X_test_s[:,0], X_test_s[:,1], c=y_test, cmap="RdBu_r", edgecolors="k", s=30) axes[1].set_title("Decision Boundary — Moons Dataset") axes[1].set_xlabel("x₁"); axes[1].set_ylabel("x₂") plt.tight_layout(); plt.show() ffn.eval() with torch.no_grad(): acc = ((ffn(X_te) > 0.5).float() == y_te).float().mean() print(f"Test accuracy: {acc:.3f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-9-output-1.png){width=1142 height=470} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Test accuracy: 0.908 ``` ::: ::: ### 🗺️ Final Architecture — The Complete Feedforward Network ```{=html}
``` --- ## Summary: The Complete Journey | Step | What we added | New concept | |------|--------------|-------------| | 1 | Single input, single output | Weight, bias, MSE | | 2 | Multiple inputs | Weight vector, dot product | | 3 | Sigmoid output | Activation function, cross-entropy | | 4 | Single neuron | The perceptron | | 5 | Multiple neurons in parallel | Dense layer, weight matrix | | 6 | Multiple layers | Depth, representation learning | | 7 | Training | Loss, backpropagation, gradient descent | | 8 | Complete network | Feedforward neural network | A deep neural network is nothing more than **linear regression, repeated and bent**. The non-linearities (activation functions) are what give it expressive power; the depth is what gives it the ability to learn hierarchical representations. Everything else is engineering. --- ## Further Reading - Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville — *Deep Learning* (free online) - Nielsen — *Neural Networks and Deep Learning* (free online) - PyTorch official tutorials: - Karpathy's *Neural Networks: Zero to Hero* series on YouTube
